text.title: Platform Differences, Gestures, and Progressive Enhancement
text.summary: iOS vs Android design differences, standard gesture vocabulary, progressive enhancement tiers, and mobile accessibility requirements.

rule.statement: Design platform-specific interaction patterns where they differ: iOS uses bottom tabs and swipe-from-edge back navigation; Android uses bottom nav bar and back gesture or button. Never assume one platform's gesture behavior on the other.
rule.rationale: Users have trained gestures from their OS. Swipe-from-left-edge is iOS back navigation; on Android it may trigger a drawer. Building cross-platform patterns without accounting for these differences causes gesture conflicts.

list.item: iOS: bottom tab bar, back via left-edge swipe, sheets from bottom, 44pt targets
Android: bottom navigation, back via gesture or system button, full-screen dialogs, 48dp targets
iOS: right side for primary actions; Android: Floating Action Button for primary creation action

rule.statement: Use only standard platform gestures. Always provide a visible alternative (button or link) for every gesture-based action. Never invent non-standard gestures without visible affordances.
rule.rationale: Custom gestures are undiscoverable. Users have no way to know they exist without explicit hints. Standard gestures work because users have learned them from the OS; novel gestures require onboarding that most users skip.

list.item: Long press: context menu or selection mode
Swipe horizontal: navigate between views or reveal row actions
Pull down: refresh (always pair with a visible refresh button)

rule.statement: Build mobile as the baseline with core functionality and touch-optimization. Add tablet enhancements (side panels, more visible content). Add desktop enhancements (full navigation, multi-column, keyboard shortcuts) only after mobile baseline is complete.
rule.rationale: Enhancement tiers prevent mobile from being treated as a stripped-down desktop version. The mobile tier is complete and functional on its own; larger screens receive additive enhancements, not different products.

rule.statement: Support VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android). Respect system text-size settings — never use absolute units that break at 150-200% text scale. Respect prefers-reduced-motion for animations.
rule.rationale: 15% of users rely on screen readers at some point. Large text size settings are common among older users and low-vision users. Breaking text scaling or ignoring reduced-motion preferences excludes significant user populations.

antipattern.description: Shrinking desktop design to fit mobile viewport instead of redesigning for mobile context
antipattern.reason: Produces tiny touch targets, cramped layouts, and forced-zoom content. Mobile is a different context requiring different information hierarchy, not a smaller copy of the desktop layout.

antipattern.description: Skeleton screens replaced with generic spinners for content-heavy pages
antipattern.reason: Skeleton screens reduce perceived load time for known-layout content because they set accurate expectations. Generic spinners with unknown duration increase perceived wait times and anxiety.
